ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Banh Mi; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class. Banh Mi with egg and salad is a filled baguette-style sandwich.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sandwiches and filled rolls) is the closest defensible category for Banh Mi. No specific Banh Mi code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Banh Mi or Vietnamese sandwich; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 Banh Mi sandwich with a side salad
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one complete Banh Mi sandwich with a side salad, weighing approximately 350 g, comprising a crispy baguette filled with one sunny side up egg, fresh salad vegetables, pickled daikon and carrot, coriander, chilli, mayonnaise, and soy sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg, whole, cooked, fried; Bread, french or vienna (includes sourdough); Salad vegetables composite
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Vegetarian Banh Mi with Sunny Side Up Egg. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: fried egg (FDC ID 173424), French bread (FDC ID 172687), and mixed salad vegetables with condiments. The input record's per-100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA component assembly and found broadly consistent. Values are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not contain a direct Banh Mi entry. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the component assembly does not yield reliable composite values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; White bread / French baguette analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegetarian Banh Mi with Sunny Side Up Egg. The GI of 70 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: French baguette / white bread, which has a published GI of approximately 70 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The egg and salad components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 55.0) / 100 = 38.5, rounded to 38.6. Carbohydrate per serving = 15.7 g/100g × 350 g = 54.95 g ≈ 55.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 271 mg per 100 g; estimated 949 mg per full 350 g serving, approaching 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and mayonnaise, limit the pickled vegetable component, and consider consuming only half a serving to keep sodium intake within a single-meal target of approximately 600–800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (≈70) from refined white baguette; glycemic load per full serving is approximately 38.6, which is high and will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the baguette portion to half or substitute with a wholegrain roll to reduce glycemic load, pair with the egg and salad components which slow gastric emptying, and monitor postprandial blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 108.6 mg per 100 g (approximately 380 mg per full serving) from the sunny side up egg; saturated fat is low at 2.0 g per 100 g; sodium is borderline high at 271 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that one full serving provides approximately 380 mg dietary cholesterol, exceeding the commonly cited 300 mg daily threshold; limiting to half a serving or consuming less frequently is advisable, and sodium reduction through condiment modification is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chilli (nightshade) may exacerbate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals; mayonnaise may contain pro-inflammatory vegetable oils; sodium from soy sauce may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced chilli, soy sauce, and mayonnaise; the egg provides vitamin D and choline with mild anti-inflammatory properties, and fresh vegetables contribute antioxidants including vitamin C at 5.7 mg per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sunny Side Up Egg Banh Mi with Salad (Vegetarian) — known in Vietnamese as Bánh Mì Trứng Ốp La Kèm Salad (Chay) and in Chinese as 太阳蛋越南法棍配沙拉（素食）— is a Vietnamese-origin vegetarian sandwich served in Singapore. It consists of a crispy, light French baguette (bánh mì) split lengthwise and filled with one or two pan-fried sunny side up eggs, fresh lettuce, sliced cucumber, julienned carrot, pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), fresh coriander, sliced chilli, mayonnaise, and soy sauce. The baguette is baked until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft inside, a hallmark of Vietnamese-French culinary fusion. The egg is pan-fried in a small amount of oil until the white is set and the yolk remains runny. The combination of textures — crispy bread, silky egg yolk, crunchy fresh and pickled vegetables — and flavours — savoury, tangy, mildly spicy — makes this a satisfying and balanced light meal. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 157 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 8.0 g fat (saturated fat 2.0 g), 15.7 g carbohydrate, 2.3 g sugar, 1.4 g dietary fibre, 271 mg sodium, and 108.6 mg cholesterol. A standard serving of 350 g provides approximately 550 kcal and 949 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high) due to the refined white baguette, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 38.6 per serving — a clinically significant consideration for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The dish is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to egg and mayonnaise. It is not gluten-free. Clinically, the sodium content warrants caution for hypertension, the cholesterol content warrants caution for cardiovascular disease, and the high glycemic load warrants portion control for diabetes management.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Vegetarian Banh Mi with Sunny Side Up Egg in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried egg, French bread, and salad vegetables, cross-checked against the input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the French baguette/white bread GI published in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese sandwich dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Component-level assembly does not yield reliable composite values for these fields. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for the full composite dish is not reliably available from approved sources for this preparation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite dish are not available as a validated single-entry figure in any approved source consulted (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA) as of 2024. 4. The input record listed the HPB Singapore Foodo URL (https://foodo.sg/) as a source but the support type was 'menu_listing' only, not nutrient data; this source was therefore not used as a nutrient evidence source. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.06 g per 100 g is retained from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with small amounts of partially hydrogenated oil in commercial mayonnaise and is not confirmed zero — it is a plausible trace value. 6. The dish is sold under menu code BM4 at a GrabFood-listed Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as egg provenance and mayonnaise brand are unknown. 8. Total flat aliases: 15 entries. No discrepancy found.
